Resolution:
This House would live by Nietzsche’s doctrine of eternal recurrence.
INFO SLIDE: The philosopher Freidrich Nietzsche grappled with how to live a meaningful life if you do not believe in religion nor in objective morality. He proposed a thought experiment: imagine you were forced to live your life over and over again. Instead of nonexistence or an afterlife, you will watch as you live the exact same life, make the exact same choices, and get the exact same result over and over. This is eternal recurrence. Nietzsche says you ought to live in such a way that you could face eternal recurrence with no regrets, no wish to have done things differently.

Resolution:

INFOSLIDE: Under US law, the Johnson Amendment forbids all non-profit organizations from attempting to influence the outcome of elections. This includes, but is not limited to, a prohibition on endorsing or opposing candidates, and a prohibition on making campaign contributions. They are, however, allowed to engage in certain facially nonpartisan activities like holding public forums and get-out-the-vote drives. For clarity, the Johnson amendment applies to churches, universities, secular nonprofits like the ACLU, and non-profit sports entities like the NCAA.

Resolution: THBT the investment of private equity firms into healthcare has done more harm than good.
THBT governments should prioritize cash handouts as opposed to social services (e.g. subsidized housing, free healthcare, food stamps) when providing welfare.
THBT governments should prioritize universal access to healthcare over the protection of intellectual property rights for pharmaceutical companies.
THO celebrities taking creative roles that are traditionally filled by non-celebrity specialists (e.g. podcast hosting, voice acting, fashion design, audiobook narration, etc.).

THR BookTok.
BookTok refers to creators on the short form media platform TikTok making content about books. The videos share reviews, recommendations, and discussions of books. The videos are typically more entertaining than other forms of book reviews.

THBT the 'founder hero' culture has done more harm than good.
The "founder hero" culture is the phenomenon of glorification of startup founders as almost mythical visionary figures, seen as the main drivers of innovation and social progress. Notable examples include Elon Musk (Tesla, SpaceX), Steve Jobs (Apple), Mark Zuckerberg (Facebook), and Elizabeth Holmes (Theranos).

THO the pursuit of 'unicorn' status in startups.
In the context of startups, the term 'unicorn' refers to prestigious startups that are valued at over $1 billion.
TH, as a new graduate entrepreneur, W prioritize capturing venture capital funding as opposed to self-funding and attracting customers naturally.

TH, as China, W place radical restrictions on money outflows (i.e. heavily limiting the exchange of RMBs to other currencies, placing stricter controls on international remittances, abolishing Chinese insurance on assets overseas, placing higher taxes on MNCs operating in China, etc.).
THBT post Brexit Northern Ireland should leave the United Kingdom and merge with the Republic of Ireland.

THBT European political parties should adopt Cordon Sanitaire.
A Cordon Sanitaire policy is an informal commitment to exclude extremist political parties from forming coalitions. Examples include the exclusion of the Front National in France, True Finns (The Finnish Far Right) from Finland, or the call to ban far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD).

In the aftermath of a nuclear apocalypse, THW establish a new society based on the principles of the "Noble Lie".
The "Noble Lie" is a political philosophy suggesting that the state can use propaganda to maintain social harmony and serve its interests. It posits that individuals hold a shared “noble” falsehood, and that society can only function if many people believe in such a lie.

THP a world where people who care about creating change in the world follow the 'earn to give model' instead of 'working to maximize social good'.
The "earn-to-give model" is a philosophy/movement that suggests the best strategy for people who want to create a change in the world is to earn a substantial income and then donate a significant portion of it to cost- effective charities. On the other hand, "work that maximizes social good" focuses on how you can create a positive impact on people through your work in various ways, such as social entrepreneurship or working in non-profit organizations. Usually, those working to maximize social good choose sectors that are less profitable and earn less money than the earn-to-give model.

THP the Jedi philosophy to the Sith philosophy.
In Star Wars, certain individuals are born with the power to access the 'Force' -- an energy field that binds the universe together. A Force-sensitive individual can levitate objects, trick minds, and sometimes predict the short-term future. The Jedi believe that the best way to control the Force is by suppressing your emotions, and so follow a philosophy of detachment from the living world (including rejecting attachments such as romantic relationships). The Sith believe in encouraging strong emotion and using it to fuel the Force. Both seek peace in the galaxy.

THBT countries with high rare earth mineral deposits (e.g., lithium, cobalt) should nationalize its extraction and production.
Rare earth minerals have diverse uses. Lithium and cobalt, for instance, can be used in making batteries, as in smartphones or electric vehicles (EVs).

THP a world where China has independently developed the 7 nanometre process.
The 7 nanometre process refers to the ability to manufacture semiconductor technology that is 7nm in size. Huawei's latest smartphone is reported to include 7nm process semiconductors. If true, this would prove China's ability to independently develop this technology, despite sanctions from the United States to prevent China from doing so. Currently, only South Korea and Taiwan can develop semiconductors smaller than 7nm to commercial scale. Semiconductors are a critical part of modern electronics (e.g computers, smartphones etc).
THR smartphones.

THP the carbon tax system to the carbon cap-and-trade system.
A carbon tax is a fixed fee imposed on carbon emissions, providing a direct financial incentive for businesses to reduce their carbon footprint. In contrast, a cap-and-trade system sets a maximum limit (cap) on total emissions and allows companies to buy, sell, or trade emission permits, creating a market-driven approach to reducing pollution.

For health care emergencies requiring collective action (e.g. pandemics, pollution-related illness, etc.), THW grant the WHO the ability to implement international laws that override sovereign laws.
The World Health Organization (WHO) is a specialized agency of the United Nations responsible for international public health. Its mission is to promote health, respond to global health emergencies, and address major health challenges. The WHO provides guidelines, research, and technical support to help countries strengthen their health systems. Currently, the WHO has no enforcement powers and relies on countries voluntarily following its recommendations.

THS green central banking.
Green central banking refers to central banks deliberately using their monetary policy instruments to incentivize climate-friendly market activities. For instance, targeted green lending programmes, targeted green bond purchase programmes, ""brown penalties"" (higher interest rates for pollution-intensive banks or businesses), lowering the financing rate for banks that offer collateral with a smaller carbon footprint, and mandatory disclosures of climate risks. For the purposes of the debate, green central banking would support climate-friendly policies at the expense of traditional monetary objectives such as targeting inflation rates, ensuring financial stability and market efficiency, as well as retaining neutrality of intervention.

THBT the US should curb the rapid growth of data centers.
Data centers are crucial physical infrastructures which stores large amounts of digital information that fuel emerging technologies such as AI and the Internet of Things. Currently, there are over 5000 data centers across the United States which are primarily developed for Big Tech companies like Google, Meta, Microsoft and Amazon. While data centers have brought billions of dollars in investments and created thousands of jobs, they are extremely energy-intensive and use immense amounts of local resources especially water.

THP the legislative review model to the judicial supremacy model in liberal democracies.
Constitutional review is the set of legal mechanisms used to verify whether laws, normative acts, or decisions of public authorities comply with a country's Constitution. There are two institutional models widely adopted by liberal democracies: (i) In the legislative override model, parliament can, through a formal process typically requiring a qualified majority such as two-thirds of votes, declare that a certain law will remain valid even after having been deemed unconstitutional by a court. This decision must be renewed periodically or is suspended. An example of this model is Canada, which adopts this approach through the so-called "notwithstanding clause" (Section 33 of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms). (ii) In the judicial supremacy model, decisions issued by constitutional courts, especially supreme courts, are final in constitutional matters. The Legislature can respond politically, such as by approving constitutional amendments or altering ordinary laws, but does not possess formal mechanisms to directly nullify these decisions. This model is characteristic of the United States, where only a new decision by the Supreme Court can modify an interpretation previously established by the Court.
TH, as the Democratic Party, S the rise of left wing conspiracy theories (e.g. that the 2024 election was stolen from Kamala Harris, that Donald Trump is being controlled by Russia, etc.)

TH, as the culinary community in the Global South, W oppose the Michelinization of local restaurants.
Michelinization is the process by which local culinary practices, dishes, or street foods are evaluated, transformed, or standardized according to elite, globalized culinary standards, typically associated with Michelin guides but not limited to them.

THBT animal rights advocacy should be targeted at people to change their preference for meat, rather than at corporations/businesses to adopt close meat substitutes (e.g., convincing restaurants to sell plant-based meat, funding research into cell-based meat alternatives).
THP a world where fine dining quality recognition (e.g. Michelin stars, James Beard awards) is evaluated solely on food quality as opposed to a holistic experience.

THW make secondary school (ages 12 to 18) mathematics education focus on statistics (e.g. probability, data analysis) as opposed to calculus (e.g. algebra, functions, differentiation).

THBT the intellectualization of romance does more harm than good.
The intellectualization of romance refers to the tendency to rationalize romantic interest or relationships as opposed to attempting to face emotional vulnerability or express emotions authentically. Instances of this include gamifying interactions with your love interest, deliberately avoiding communicating emotions in the fear that it appears like a "concession," or overly analyzing trivial interactions in search of positive signaling. Examples of media that depict the intellectualization of romance include Kaguya-Sama: Love is War and the Love Hypothesis, franchises that emphasize logical reasoning in the face of emotional vulnerability as opposed to acknowledging the scope or intensity of one’s feelings.

THO the gamification of romantic love in pop culture.
Gamification of love is the popularisation of reality TV shows that depict romantic love as competitions or experiments(e.g. shows like Love Island and Date Rush)
THR the narrative that individuals should do what they love.

Editorial independence refers to the freedom of journalists and media organizations to make content decisions (including what to report, who to report, and how to report it) without pressure and/or censorship from the government. Examples of State-Controlled Media Organizations are People’s Television (Philippines), China Central Television Network, and Russia Today. International Humanitarian Law protects journalists as civilians during armed conflicts. This means they cannot be deliberately targeted by combatants and must be treated humanely if captured. IHL protections are conditional: journalists who take direct part in hostilities or act as combatants may lose their civilian protection.
THBT journalists working for state-controlled media outlets lacking editorial independence should not be granted protection under International Humanitarian Law when reporting during armed conflicts.
Topic Committee: Shayor Khan, Divit Chopra, Derek Chang, Milla Chavez, Zachary Siegel, Alex Parikh-Briggs, Justin Zhou, Collin Goel, Kalani Gaviola (equity)

“Moneyball” refers to the use of statistical analysis in sports to evaluate players, guide recruitment, and optimize strategies. Resolution:
The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) is a United States Federal Agency that is responsible for the control and supervision of various food and medical products. Specifically in regards to drug safety, the FDA requires drugs go through an approval process which consists of five steps: discovery/concept, preclinical research, clinical research, FDA review, and FDA post-market safety monitoring. For the purposes of this debate, increasing testing requirements means increasing the length and due-diligence performed during each step as well as adding longer term testing on human subjects before the post-market safety monitoring step and potentially adding additional steps before the drug is launched in the open market. 
THW increase the FDA clinical testing requirements for drugs in which the primary purpose is to change brain chemistry (e.g. Ozempic, Antidepressants)

Resolution:
This house regrets the romanticization of science-based fitness.
Science-based fitness refers to the application of training, nutrition, and recovery practices that are grounded in aggregated research and scientific evidence on the most effective ways to achieve health and fitness goals. This approach emphasises diligent tracking of all calories consumed, eating in a caloric deficit, designing workout routines with optimal splits, consuming 1.5 to 2 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight, and consistently training with progressive overload. Additionally, it discourages the use of performance-enhancing drugs Prominent proponents of science-based fitness include Jeff Nippard, Dr. Mike Israetel, and Eric Helms.

Panda diplomacy refers to China's practice of sending giant pandas to other countries, primarily as a tool of diplomacy and wildlife conservation. Initially, pandas were gifted, but since 1984, they have been leased due to a policy change. This strategy is a form of soft power, aiming to foster positive international relations and boost China's image abroad by leveraging the pandas' universal appeal. In 2024, Malaysia's Plantation and Commodities Minister Johari Abdul Ghani had suggested giving orangutans to major palm oil importers to bolster relations.

Resolution:
This House Opposes the norm to prefer the natural to the artificial.
The “natural” is typically understood as something occurring in nature without human intervention (e.g., fruits, ecosystems, unmodified genes, untreated materials). The “artificial” is something created, modified, or enhanced by humans (e.g., synthetic drugs, GMOs, lab-grown foods, engineered environments, or digital creations). Many cultures and ethical frameworks valorize the “natural” over the “artificial,” associating it with purity, authenticity, and health. This motion asks whether that preference should be challenged.

Resolution:
This House supports the "Bad Man" approach in policy making.
The term "bad man" in legal theory refers to a hypothetical individual who is solely concerned with the practical consequences of legal rules, particularly the potential for punishment, rather than with moral or ethical considerations. This "bad man" is interested in predicting how the law will be applied to their actions, focusing on the material consequences like fines or imprisonment, rather than on whether those actions are morally right or wrong.

You do not qualify for aid; you can afford both options without loans, though the private school would cut into your retirement savings.

Resolution:
Medical examiners in the US should determine a person’s Cause of Death, but not their Manner of Death.
INFO SLIDE: Cause of Death refers to the immediate biological reason why someone died, for example blood loss or brain damage. Manner of Death refers to the circumstances leading to the biological cause. There are five recognized findings for manner of death: homicide, suicide, accident, natural causes, and undetermined. In the US, medical examiners commonly report both the cause and manner of death. The manner of death sometimes determines whether a death is investigated by the government. In a trial, the medical examiner’s findings are commonly used as evidence. Expert witnesses with different opinions may testify. In other countries, medical examiners report a cause of death, but not a manner of death.

Resolution:
THR the rise of beli.
Beli is a social media app where users are able to rank/review restaurants and view the restaurant activity of “Friends”. Beli uses a series of pairwise comparisons — asking the user to select their preference between the given restaurant and previously-ranked restaurants — to establish ratings. Furthermore, with the exception of reviews from “Friends”, only user ratings, not reviews, are publicly displayed on the restaurant’s page. It currently has more than a million active users, mostly centralized in North America.

Resolution:
THW not reallocate seats.
Info slide: The Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP), led by Prime Minister Narendra Modi, currently does not hold an absolute majority in the Lok Sabha, but retains a majority through the National Democratic Alliance (NDA) coalition with other parties. The BJP has been criticized for their Hindu nationalist, far-right ideology, as well as spreading disinformation about and demonizing Muslims. Under the BJP, India has experienced significant democratic backsliding. Modi and the BJP have traditionally faced resistance from the wealthy, highly-educated southern states (e.g. Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Kerala, Tamil Nadu and Telangana), whereas they are more popular in the North. The southern states currently only account for 20% of India’s total population. The Lok Sabha is the lower house of India’s Parliament, with seats allocated to each state based on their population size in 1971. However, the number of seats per state has been frozen since then to avoid punishing states like Kerala and Tamil Nadu for highly effective family planning programs that have resulted in lower population sizes. The Lok Sabha seat freeze is set to expire in 2026.
Topic Committee: Zach Berg (chair), Giselle Paulson, Ambika Grover, Devan Tatlow, Shashwat Rai, Maria Xu (equity), Darshanik Aryal (equity)

Regardless of funding source, Institutional Review Boards (IRBs) at research institutions (e.g., universities, hospitals) enforce ethical standards to prevent inhumane or exploitative experiments.

Resolution:
As Julius Caesar, this House would cross the Rubicon River.
INFO SLIDE: In 49 BCE, Julius Caesar was proconsul of Gaul, commanding a loyal army and holding immunity from prosecution while in office. During this time, he had also gained wealth through conquest, broken many laws, and made powerful enemies in the Roman Senate. The Senate ordered Caesar to dismiss his army and return to Rome. Obeying would end his command and immunity, leaving him vulnerable to prosecution and political defeat. The little Rubicon River marked the boundary between his province and Italy. Roman law forbade any general from crossing into Italy with an army. Doing so would defy the Senate’s authority and risk civil war, but it would also give Caesar the chance to preserve and expand his power.
Topic Committee: Evan Streams (chair)

Resolution:
In market economies, This House prefers broad-based, value-added taxes over import tariffs.
INFO SLIDE: A value-added tax (VAT) is a broad-based consumption tax applied at each stage of production on both domestic and imported goods. An import tariff is a tax on goods brought into a country, often used to protect domestic industries or influence trade. Both raise revenue, but VATs tax overall consumption, while tariffs target imports specifically.
Topic Committee: Evan Streams (chair)

Resolution:
In the U.S., This House would prohibit housing restrictions based on age.
INFO SLIDE: Under U.S. federal law, it is illegal to restrict housing based on characteristics such as race, sex, religion, or disability. However, current law permits housing communities to limit residency based on age. For example, Massachusetts has over 150 housing developments that are designated for senior citizens only.
Topic Committee: Evan Streams (chair)

Resolution:
This House regrets the human tendency to ascribe moral purpose to suffering.
INFO SLIDE: The tendency to ascribe moral purpose to suffering refers to the belief that pain, loss, or hardship have inherent moral or spiritual meaning, such as serving as a punishment, a test, or a path to personal growth. This belief appears across many cultures and religions, from ideas of divine testing or karma, to sayings like “everything happens for a reason” or “no pain, no gain.”
Topic Committee: Evan Streams (chair)

Resolution:
This House would replace traditional property taxes in the United States with Harberger taxes.
INFO SLIDE: Under the current U.S. system, property taxes are based on government-assessed values. A Harberger tax instead requires owners to self-report the value of their property. That declared value is public, and if someone offers to purchase the property at or above that value, the owner must sell.
Topic Committee: Evan Streams (chair)

Resolution:
This House prefers a world with karmic reincarnation.
INFO SLIDE: Karmic reincarnation is the idea that after death, individuals are reborn into a new life whose quality reflects the moral conduct of their past life. People do not retain memories of previous lives. While versions of this concept appear in several religious traditions, this description reflects a simplified model rather than any full theological account.
Topic Committee: Evan Streams (chair)

Resolution:
This House opposes the ideal of historical objectivity.
INFO SLIDE: The ideal of historical objectivity holds that historians can describe the past “as it actually happened” (a phrase associated with 19th-century historian Leopold von Ranke) through impartial analysis of evidence. Critics, including postmodern and critical historians, argue that all history reflects the historian’s perspective, social context, and values, making true objectivity unattainable and potentially misleading.
Topic Committee: Evan Streams (chair)

Resolution:
This House supports the teleological view of history.
INFO SLIDE: The teleological view of history holds that historical events move toward an identifiable end or purpose: for example, greater freedom, technological advancement, or the realization of a particular social system. By contrast, the contingent view of history holds that historical outcomes are primarily determined by chance and individual circumstances, rather than by an inevitable direction or goal.
Topic Committee: Evan Streams (chair)

Resolution:
This House prefers genealogy as the primary method of history.
INFO SLIDE: Genealogical history, as developed by thinkers such as Michel Foucault, examines how ideas, practices, and institutions emerge through complex and often uneven historical processes. Unlike traditional or narrative history, which typically presents events in a linear sequence centered on major figures and cohesive storylines, genealogical history focuses more on discontinuities and power relations.
Topic Committee: Evan Streams (chair)

The concept highlights a tension within activism between maintaining social comfort and confronting discriminatory behavior.

Resolution:

INFO: In the popular game Dungeons & Dragons, the Dungeon Master (DM) runs the game world and adjudicates the rules. Players often use the same characters over long campaigns that develop a shared story over months or years. For the purposes of this debate round, you are the DM, and all of the characters in a year-long campaign are very likely to die in an impending routine battle, due to both bad luck and poor decisions by the players. If they all die, there is no way to bring them back, effectively ending the game. As the DM, you could “fudge” – which means bend, break or ignore the rules to help them survive, thereby continuing the game.

Resolution:
THBT activist short selling does more good than harm to financial markets.
Short selling is an investment strategy where an investor borrows an asset, such as company shares, and sells it on the open market with the expectation that its price will decline. The goal is to buy the asset back at a lower price in the future, return the borrowed shares, and profit from the difference. Activist short selling involves investors shorting an asset, and then publicly disseminating negative information about the asset (e.g., fraud and accounting manipulation, overvaluation) to deliberately drive down its share price.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Alexander Georgatos, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:

“Internationalizing” a currency means adopting policies intended to increase the use of that currency by nonresidents in reserve holdings, cross-border trade settlement, and cross-border financial transactions.

Resolution:
THP the North American model over the European model in developing young athletes.
The North American model develops young athletes primarily through school and university sports programs. Athletes compete in interscholastic leagues and, at the tertiary level, through the NCAA (National Collegiate Athletic Association), which offers athletic scholarships. Young athletes are typically drafted after graduation and sign professional contracts with teams. The European model develops athletes primarily through sports clubs where players are recruited young, trained by professional volunteers, and may be promoted based on sporting merit, which may result in signing professional contracts in their teens. Young players are typically amateurs and from the grassroots, and training is largely separate from formal education.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Alexander Georgatos, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:
Assuming feasibility, THW amend Article 5 of the North Atlantic Treaty to include the principle of automaticity.
NATO Article 5 stipulates that members will take action they deem necessary, including armed force, to assist a member under attack, but does not guarantee automatic military intervention. It provides a commitment to collective defense ("an attack on one is an attack on all") without imposing a mandatory, automatic, or predetermined military response. Adding automaticity would require NATO members to provide immediate military assistance if any member is attacked.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Alexander Georgatos, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:
THBT Middle Powers will prefer the world order of the 2010s to the world order of the 2040s.
Middle powers are states that are influential within the international system, but do not have the power to be considered superpowers. Examples include Canada, India, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Brazil, Singapore, and Turkey. A “world order” is understood as the dominant framework of laws, norms, and institutions that guide relations between states concerning trade, conflict resolution, and power distribution. The 2010s world order was characterized by U.S. global primacy, relatively stable institutions like NATO and the WTO, deep economic globalization, predictable security guarantees, and the rise of China as a global superpower and competitor to the U.S.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Alexander Georgatos, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:
THR Collier’s reframing of the cause of ethnic conflict.
Some recent IR scholars such as Paul Collier have attempted to reframe the causes of civil wars as short term, based on statistics linking poor economic conditions to civil conflict and the absence of civil war in certain countries with strong ethnic tensions. They argue that the true causes of civil war are rooted in immediate economic conditions rather than longstanding ethnic conflict. Under this view, wars may appear to be ethnically motivated, but likely because leaders of insurgent groups stoke ethnic conflict in order to gain support for their side.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Alexander Georgatos, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Some examples for "Games as a service" include Fortnite, Helldivers 2, World of Warcraft, Anthem, Concord, Highguard, etc.

Resolution:
In common law systems, THS the expansion of duty to rescue laws to require individuals to take minimal reasonable steps (e.g., calling emergency services) to assist persons in imminent danger, assuming they would not endanger themselves or the person in danger in the process of doing so.
In common law, a "duty to rescue" means that you will be legally punished for failing to aid a person in peril. Currently, there is a legal duty to rescue only in cases where a) you are responsible for the hazard or b) you have a special relationship with the person in peril (e.g. parents, babysitters, employers).
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Alexander Georgatos, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:
THBT evidence obtained by a private individual should be held to the same standard in criminal trials as evidence obtained by the state.
In the status quo, evidence submitted by private individuals to a court is not held to the same standards as evidence submitted by the state. For instance, if a private citizen performs what would be an illegal search and seizure, the evidence remains admissible in further criminal proceedings. Someone who is paid by the government, or who has gotten permission from the government beforehand, or supplies information routinely, is considered a government agent, and their evidence is still excluded under the exclusionary rule.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Alexander Georgatos, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:
THP the qualified approach to double jeopardy over the absolute approach.
Absolute double jeopardy prevents the same government from trying an individual again for the same offence once a person is acquitted or convicted. Qualified double jeopardy allows the same government to try them again if there was either 1) a serious legal error or 2) substantial new evidence is discovered.
If someone is found guilty and later discovers new evidence proving their innocence, seeking a new trial is generally not considered double jeopardy, but rather a request for post-conviction relief (like a direct appeal or writ of habeas corpus).
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Alexander Georgatos, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:

A technocratic cabinet is a government composed of non-political experts, such as scientists, economists, and academics, who are appointed based on their specialized technical knowledge.
A loyalist cabinet is a government formed of the leader's closest political allies, party members, and long-time supporters who are appointed based on their personal or professional devotion.

These kingdoms would compete with each other politically, economically and militarily. Resolution:
THP a world where limitarianism was the dominant form of governance.
Limitarianism is a political philosophy most closely associated with Ingrid Robeyns, who argues that there should be an upper moral and legal limit to how much wealth any individual can accumulate. Typically, surpassing the limit would be understood as possessing wealth beyond what is necessary to lead a fully flourishing life. In Robeyns’ work, particularly Limitarianism: The Case Against Extreme Wealth, she argues that the state is justified to collect the individuals' wealth that exceeds the limit.

Resolution:
THBT The Cosby Show should have dealt with more serious themes (e.g., HIV, Vietnam, economic oppression of Black people, etc.)
The Cosby Show aired on NBC from 1984 to 1992 and became one of the most influential sitcoms in American television history. Created by and starring Bill Cosby, the show followed the Huxtable family, an upper-middle-class Black family living in Brooklyn, New York City. At a time when many television portrayals of Black Americans were limited or stereotypical, the show was widely praised for presenting a successful, professional Black family—Cliff Huxtable as a doctor and Clair Huxtable as a lawyer. Most episodes focused on everyday family life, school, relationships, and humor rather than major social or political issues.

Colonial determinism refers to the idea that postcolonial states are irredeemably shaped or influenced by the structures of colonial rule, and that these structures are primarily responsible for the economic, political, and social contexts these states find themselves in.

Resolution:
TH, as the Black Panther Party starting a new Peoples' Free Medical Clinic in 1972, W accept government funds.
The Black Panther Party (BPP) was a revolutionary Black-led organization founded in 1966 that focused on fighting racial oppression, police brutality, and economic inequality in the United States. Their activism often put them in direct conflict with law enforcement and federal agencies, which saw them as a threat. While they are often remembered for their armed self-defense—The Panthers openly carried firearms and organized armed patrols to monitor police activity, especially in Black neighborhoods, arguing that this was a legal way to protect residents from police abuse—a major part of their work was building community programs that directly supported Black communities. These included free breakfast programs for children, education initiatives, and others. In 1972, the BPP added "completely free healthcare" to their platform, and any new branch of the BPP was required to operate a Peoples' Free Medical Clinic (PFMC) that provided medical services, legal counseling, and political education. PFMCs were generally operated on donations and volunteers. PFMCs were harassed by city health inspectors, local police, and the FBI. Later, federal grants were offered to PFMCs as part of the War on Poverty.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Alexander Georgatos, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Following the Rwandan genocide, Rwanda instituted a ban on 'ethnic classification.' This law made it illegal with the exception of very particular 'non-divisive' contexts (eg. academic analysis of the genocide) to publicly identify yourself or others as a member of a particular ethnic group (eg. Hutu or Tutsi), and removed ethnic identity on all identification documents, replacing it with the sole identification of 'Rwandan'.

Resolution:
THP the UK system to the US for college admissions.
The UK system only allows applicants to apply to five schools. Admissions are primarily based on grades and test scores, with circumstances and written materials considered secondary. If students do not receive an admission offer by summer, a ‘clearing’ period starts where students can reapply to schools with open seats still available. Students must also apply directly to a major.
The US system has no limit to the number of schools students can apply to. Admissions are ‘holistic’, considering extracurriculars, essays, grades, and test scores, as well as circumstances in the application process. In addition, there are decision rounds, where students can apply early to schools. These sometimes include binding agreements, where students must attend the university if admitted.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Dari Zell, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:

Medicine, Engineering and Law are the most commonly selected degrees by high-ranking entrants, due to their high pay and/or associated social standing. As a consequence of this social norm, many less lucrative fields and occupations such as teaching, agriculture, scientific research, environmental conservation, social sciences etc. have lagged behind in their advancement, since most highly educated graduates opt into mainstream jobs.

These services do not require an examination of customers' incomes or credit histories. In 2025, buy-now-pay-later services accounted for $560bn in global transactions.

Resolution:
THBT it is in Formula One's interest to introduce aggressive Balance-of-Performance.
In Formula One racing, the specifics of a car can have a significant impact on how fast drivers can go, allowing teams to dominate via engineering advancements. Drivers determine the margins, still having enough impact for drivers to distinguish themselves in a given season, but sometimes not enough to overcome engineering differences.
Balance-of-Performance measures are regulations and mechanisms that maintain parity between competing racing cars by adjusting limits on the more dominant car's parameters (e.g. adding ballast, limiting engine fuel flow). Right now, Formula One has some regulations to increase competitiveness, but they still allow for significant differences in performance among cars.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Dari Zell, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
TH, as a civil rights organization during the civil rights movement, W align their values with those of organizations such as the Black Panthers rather than those of the SCLC and Martin Luther King Jr.
It is the 1960s, during the height of the civil rights movement in the United States. There were two major civil rights organizations at the time with different approaches to achieving Black liberation. The Black Panther Party emphasized armed self-defense against police brutality, Black nationalism, and revolutionary political change. The Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC), led by Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., advocated non-violent protest, integration, and cooperation with existing political institutions to achieve civil rights gains.
Note: theme: Civil Rights, Revolution, and Decolonization
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Dari Zell, Alex Doft, Arnob Mitra (equity)

Resolution:
TH, as an anti-colonial revolution in Africa in 1962, W be Marxist rather than nationalist.
The year is 1962, during a wave of anti-colonial revolutions across Africa. At the same time, the Cold War is ongoing. The United States and the Soviet Union both seek influence in post-colonial states, offering financial, military, and ideological support to aligned movements. African revolutions in this period frequently had to choose between two competing ideological frameworks when deciding how to organize—Marxism or nationalism. Marxist movements emphasized class struggle, anti-capitalism, land redistribution, and dismantling colonial economic structures (framing colonialism as an extension of global capitalism). Nationalist movements typically focused on forging a unified national identity within colonial borders and establishing a sovereign state rather than a specific economic ideology.
Note: theme: Civil Rights, Revolution, and Decolonization
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Dari Zell, Alex Doft, Arnob Mitra (equity)

Resolution:
THP Diop’s approach to African identity to Senghor's theory of Négritude during the decolonization of Senegal.
The year is 1959, during the decolonization period in Senegal. Cheikh Anta Diop and Léopold Sédar Senghor were Senegalese academics. Senghor, along with colleagues, popularized the Négritude movement: a framework of critique and literary theory that emphasized African art, poetry, and literature, positing that African identity held within it boundless imaginative power. Senghor is quoted as saying, “Emotion is Negro* and reason is Greek.” Diop publicly challenged Senghor’s conception of Négritude, arguing instead that African identity should be grounded in historical, scientific, and political achievement.
* Please note that this is a dated term and is considered offensive when used today. It should not be used in-round. It is included only in quotations for historical accuracy.
Note: theme: Civil Rights, Revolution, and Decolonization
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Dari Zell, Alex Doft, Arnob Mitra (equity)

Resolution:
TH, as Rabbi Meir, W continue studying Judaism under Acher (assuming he continues to teach it) rather than leaving him.
In the Talmud (the central text of Rabbinic Judaism, which includes rabbinic stories and debates of the early centuries), there is a famous story about a fallen sage. During the second century CE in Judea, there lived a rabbi known as Acher (“the Other”), originally Rabbi Elisha ben Abuyah. He had been a prominent and widely respected scholar, but over time, he lost his faith and abandoned core elements of Jewish practice. Resolution:
THO the increasing use of the “state of exception.”
Italian philosopher Giorgio Agamben argues that modern governments increasingly rely on states of exception—special periods where normal laws are suspended in the name of protecting the public. Examples include expanded surveillance after 9/11 through the USA PATRIOT Act, El Salvador suspending habeas corpus to combat gang violence, and COVID-19 lockdowns and travel restrictions. States of exception are also used in widely accepted situations like hurricanes or major disease outbreaks. In many countries, these powers have built-in limits—such as sunset clauses or judicial review—though in some cases they have persisted or expanded over time.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Dari Zell, Alex Doft, Arnob Mitra (equity)

Resolution:
THS United States foreign policy becoming increasingly isolationist.
For much of the mid to late 20th century and early 21st century, the United States has had a fairly interventionist foreign policy position, with military interventions in Asia, Africa and the Middle East. Under the last Presidencies, US foreign policy has shifted towards more non-interventionalist, or isolationist, positions. This included the US withdrawal from Syria beginning in 2019, the US withdrawal from Iraq beginning in 2020, and the US withdrawal from Afghanistan in 2021. Isolationism is also associated with a withdrawal from multilateral and bilateral organisations, such as the US withdrawal from the Trade Pacific Partnership, the Iran Deal and the Paris Agreement.

Resolution:
TH, as a prominent group of Black abolitionists in the antebellum North, W interpret the Constitution as an anti-slavery document rather than a pro-slavery document.
You are a prominent group of Black abolitionists in a northern US free state a few years before the Civil War (late 1850s). There are two primary ways for abolitionists to interpret the Constitution at this time. The first is to interpret the Constitution as a pro-slavery document, citing clauses such as the three-fifths compromise (which gives states three votes for every five slaves), the ban on outlawing the international slave trade until 1808, and the so-called “fugitive slave clause,” which ensures that a slave cannot legally abandon a slave state for a free state. The second is to interpret the Constitution as an anti-slavery document, arguing that it established strong principles of equality that logically apply to all people.

Resolution:
THW legalize champerty.
Champerty is a type of litigation financing where a third party (such as an investor, company, or fund) gives money to someone to help them run a lawsuit in exchange for a portion of whatever money is won. If the lawsuit fails, the funder loses the money they invested and gets nothing back. Champerty is most commonly used in class actions, where a large group of people who experienced similar harm sue a company or institution together. Many class action law firms do not charge clients upfront; instead, they take a percentage of the money won at the end. When champerty is allowed, the payment structure usually works like this: Lawyers take about 33% of the total money won. Litigation funders (the third-party investors) get paid out of that 33%. The plaintiffs—the people bringing the case—receive the remaining 66%.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Dari Zell, Alex Doft, Arnob Mitra (equity)

According to experts, export controls have put China behind the US in designing and producing cutting-edge semiconductors—but the Chinese government is investing hundreds of billions of dollars every year into China’s semiconductor ecosystem. THP a world where islamic banking is the worldwide standard of banking.

Instead of interest, Islamic banks use structures such as profit-sharing, joint ventures, and asset-based sales. Islamic banking also restricts investment in industries considered haram, such as alcohol, gambling, and pornography. Resolution:
THP a conquistadorial to a liberationist interpretation of Minecraft's gameplay in the cultural zeitgeist.
The conquistadorial interpretation of gameplay is an analytical framework in which a player’s primary actions (such as exploration, resource extraction, territorial expansion, and the defeat of major entities) are understood principally as acts of domination and control over a virtual world. Under this interpretation, progression is driven by the accumulation of success and the mastery of personal skill, rather than by immediate needs of survival or coexistence. The liberationist interpretation of gameplay understands a player’s actions as primarily oriented toward emancipation, protection, or restoration. This includes defending vulnerable groups, eliminating hostile threats, or restoring balance within the game world. This interpretation frames progression as morally justified through the removal of obstacles that constrain the freedom, safety, or autonomy of oneself or other friendly entities.

Resolution:

Wicked is a musical that reimagines the world of The Wizard of Oz by centering the story on Elphaba, later labeled the Wicked Witch of the West, and Glinda, who becomes the Good Witch. In Oz, political power is consolidated under the Wizard, who sustains his rule through propaganda, public spectacle, and the systematic scapegoating of Elphaba, while also repressing Animals who are capable of speech and independent thought. Glinda rises to prominence by aligning herself with the Wizard, gaining popularity, legitimacy, and close access to power as a symbol of goodness and stability. Although Glinda privately disagrees with many of the Wizard's actions, she chooses to remain within the system, believing that gradual reform and quiet influence are preferable to open confrontation that might destabilize Oz. Elphaba, by contrast, rejects the Wizard's legitimacy and engages in direct resistance, positioning herself as a radical challenger to the existing order and enabling the state to frame her dissent as villainy, resulting in her public vilification, isolation, and persecution even as her opposition exposes the regime's underlying injustices.

Resolution:
THP metaphorical art over visceral art as a tool for social change.
Metaphorical art uses symbolism, abstraction, or participatory elements to communicate political or social critique indirectly. An example of metaphorical art is Untitled (Portrait of Ross in L.A.), created by Felix Gonzalez-Torres during the AIDS epidemic. The work consists of a pile of colorful, individually wrapped candies weighing 175 pounds—the weight of Gonzalez-Torres’ partner, Ross, before he contracted AIDS. Visitors are encouraged to take pieces of candy, and as the pile diminishes, it mirrors Ross’s physical decline due to the illness. [image of Untitled]
Visceral art confronts viewers with graphic, emotionally intense imagery to provoke an immediate reaction. An example of visceral art is The Third of May 1808 by Francisco Goya. The painting depicts the execution of Spanish civilians by French soldiers during the Peninsular War. Goya presents the violence directly: armed soldiers, terrified civilians, and the immediacy of death. [image of The Third of May 1808]
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Alexander Georgatos, Shreya Ranjan, Dari Zell, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:
THP art without closure in transitional justice eras, as opposed to art that offers closure.
Transitional justice eras refer to specific historical and political moments in which a society is reckoning with a legacy of mass injustice, violence, or authoritarian rule—often following dictatorships, civil wars, state-sponsored repression, or colonial regimes. These periods, occurring during or shortly after political transition, are characterized by the involvement of processes such as truth commissions, war crimes trials, reparations for victims, memorialization through museums or art, and institutional reform (e.g., judiciary, police, or military). Art that offers no closure seeks to reflect the unresolved, complex, and emotionally ambiguous terrain of a society still grappling with its past. These works often reject neat moral binaries or redemptive arcs. Art that offers closure on the other hand, aims to heal, redeem, or resolve historical trauma, often providing a sense of catharsis, or restored moral order.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Alexander Georgatos, Shreya Ranjan, Dari Zell, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:
TH, as a mid-power country, W take the sign out of the window.
In a prominent January 2026 Davos address, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney declared that Canada is "taking the sign out of the window," signaling an end to upholding the "pleasant fiction" of a rules-based international order that no longer functions. Borrowing a metaphor from Václav Havel, Carney argued against continuing to believe in a broken system. Instead, he proposed a "value-based realism," urging middle powers to stop mourning the old order, confront new realities of great power rivalry, and forge new alliances. Taking the sign out of the window means breaking ties with modern superpowers. It offers the possibility of future alliance and stability, but risks the wrath of current superpowers and allies.

Resolution:
THP the ADPA model of debate to the BP model of debate.
British Parliamentary (BP) debate and American Parliamentary Debate (APDA) are the two premier parliamentary debate (“Parli”) styles at the college level. Although both styles have some similarities (there is the Government side and an Opposition side), there are three major distinctions between each style’s model of debate. First, BP debates consist of four teams, each with two debaters: Opening Government (OG), Opening Opposition (OO), Closing Government (CG), and Closing Opposition (CO). Each speaker on a team has one speech, which is 7 minutes long. The order is as following: OG, OO, OG, OO, CG, CO, CG, CO. The winner of the debate is not determined by which side wins, but by a ranked placement of all four teams from first to fourth based on their individual contributions to the round. By contrast, APDA utilizes a two-versus-two format with alternating Government and Opposition speeches (Prime Minister 7 min, Leader of Opposition 8 min, etc.) similar to the NYPDL. Second, APDA debates allow teams to run pre-prepared affirmative cases, with Government teams preparing cases/motions in advance of the round and then presenting them to the Opposition side, who has 15 minutes to prepare for them. These must give the Opposition side sufficient ground, or else the Opposition side can “tight call” the case and explain why it is skewed. In contrast, BP motions are announced shortly before the debate, and teams construct arguments extemporaneously with 15 minutes of preparation time. Third, judges in BP are explicitly instructed to prioritize “truth over tech,” meaning they should evaluate arguments based on their plausibility, comparative reasoning, and real-world logic, rather than technical concessions or dropped arguments. APDA judging is generally more flow-oriented, placing greater emphasis on technical execution, extensions, and strategic drops.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Alexander Georgatos, Shreya Ranjan, Dari Zell, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:
THR the rise of paraphrase theory in Public Forum debate.
Public Forum (PF) is a two-versus-two debate format in which each partner delivers two speeches. The speeches alternate between the affirmative (pro) and negative (con) sides on a monthly “resolution” published by the National Speech & Debate Association (NSDA). These resolutions address contemporary issues, such as “NATO should increase defense commitments to the Baltic states.” Because of this structure, PF requires extensive research and collection of evidence, with debaters writing and researching arguments for the resolution over the course of the month. At most local tournaments, debaters paraphrase their evidence in speeches rather than reading it verbatim. However, many teams competing on the national circuit—particularly at larger tournaments such as Yale and Emory—have begun exclusively reading direct quotations when introducing evidence. These quotations are drawn from “cut cards.” If opponents request to see evidence, debaters must provide these cards (often via email) so their opponents may review them during their three minutes of preparation time. Advocates of reading direct quotations argue that this practice is superior to paraphrasing and have encouraged others to adopt it as a community standard. One method of doing so is through “paraphrase theory.” In this context, a debater will argue, in a round, that the judge should penalize a team that paraphrased—by awarding them a loss or entirely dropping arguments supported by paraphrased evidence—in order to establish a deterrent norm and incentivize the use of direct quotations.
Here is an example of a “cut card”:
Ian Bremmer, GZERO, 04/19/2021, “Putin's next move won't be a Baltic invasion that could unify NATO,” https://www.gzeromedia.com/the-red-pen/putins-next-move-wont-be-a-baltic-invasion-that-could-unify-nato // MH
An attack on the Balts may be fast or it may be victorious, but probably not both. And Putin knows this. Western leaders are conflicted about the alliance, but an assault on full-fledged NATO State and EU members is exactly the kind of provocation that could awaken it. Putin understands [and] this. He hardly wants to bring the alliance together as it's eroding. Low-cost efforts to steadily undermine legitimacy and grabbing targets of opportunity when available, that is much more Putin's speed.
When reading direct quotations, debaters typically present only the highlighted portions of the evidence (including bracketed clarifications as necessary).
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Alexander Georgatos, Shreya Ranjan, Dari Zell, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:
THR the complexity of language present in philosophical writing.
In ancient Greece, philosophers like Plato wrote dialogues meant to be read aloud and debated in public, and Aristotle aimed to classify ideas clearly, even when they were complex. Starting in the late 18th century, this changed. In Immanuel Kant’s Critique of Pure Reason (1781), Kant spends pages explaining ideas using phrases like “the transcendental unity of apperception,” which essentially means the mind’s ability to recognize its experiences as belonging to one self. In Martin Heidegger’s Being and Time, he invents new words such as Dasein to mean human existence and includes atypical language. Historically, the complexity arose as philosophy developed as a field.

At some point along the journey, you realize he is alive, and are presented with the following dilemma: Reroute the spaceship to try to save Bark, forcing you to conduct several risky maneuvers to stretch the limited food and fuel you have on board and risking the lives of the three other scientists on board, or return to Earth safely as directed by NASA, and knowingly doom your stranded crew member. TH, as the mission leader, W attempt to save Bark.
Note:
theme: Personal Dilemmas [… in Space!]*
*None of these motions occur in the respective universes of the media on which they happen to be similar to. No information from those movies (outside of the information in the info slides) is necessarily applicable to the motions.

Resolution: It is the year 2154, and aliens from a planet called Pandoruh have just made contact with humanity. Climate change has made large portions of the Earth uninhabitable, but climate-based revolutions have stopped large-scale emissions and established a path for survival. The aliens are not aggressive, but they are firmly convinced that humanity should leave Earth to settle alongside them in space or risk its own destruction. Assuming the people will follow whatever decision you make, and do not know it was you who made the decision, TH, as a representative of humanity, W accept the offer.
