Resolution:
Xiao (孝) is a Confucian philosophy defined by filial piety and respect and devotion to family and elders, concepts which Confucius believed formed the foundation of all relationships.
THR society’s emphasis on Xiao, or familial devotion.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
Assuming you know your mentor is offered the same deal and the police are not lying to either of you, THW confess. 
TH is a young, up-and-coming prisoner who was caught by the police with your aged mentor. You and your mentor are brought to separate rooms, unable to communicate with each other. The officers offer you each a deal: confess to the crime you have been accused of, and receive no jail time, but your partner serves 20 years, or say nothing and serve 5 years. However, if you both confess, you must both serve 20 years together.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
Assuming nobody living knows this system exists, THBT the point system should reward/punish actions based solely on their consequences, as opposed to motivation being decisive
The Afterlife is divided into The Good Place and The Bad Place. You go to the ‘Good Place’ if you have a positive point total of actions. Currently your actions on Earth are afforded points, where every act has different point creditation. All morally good acts have positive points (saving a “life, donating to charities”, “sacrificing your share for someone else”), whereas your points get cut due to any negative actions (cheating, murder, arson, not taking POIs). Currently, if your motivations are corrupt, self-serving, you don't get points for otherwise seemingly positive actions, and vice versa. Humans while alive know the metrics on which they are judged (motivations versus consequences), but not the exact details.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
The Tragedy of the Commons is a behavioural economics dilemma that occurs when an individual must balance a protection of public resources with their own need for these goods.
THBT countries should be required to distribute life-saving technologies globally before ensuring domestic demand is fully met.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
The Allocation Dilemma occurs when there is a scarcity of resources, particularly capital, in any given firm/company, often forcing these businesses to focus on a singular area of investment.
TH, as a developing country with an agrarian economy and newly acquired resources, W focus on increasing production of their largest exports over trying to shift towards producing and exporting more advanced products (e.g. technology).
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
The Time Inconsistency Dilemma occurs when individuals must balance the time frame of their plans to address short term expectations and current events but not lose focus of anticipating future occurrences.
THS anticipatory urban planning methods in developing countries.
Anticipatory urban planning involves making decisions based on predicted future conditions such as population growth, environmental change, technological adoption, or economic shifts. It aims to shape urban development ahead of time by investing in infrastructure, setting policies, or designing districts before clear demand materializes. Both public and private sectors can be involved in these projects.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: For crimes with low prosecution and conviction rates (e.g. domestic violence, membership of criminal gangs, sexual assault), THW lower the burden of proof from 'beyond reasonable doubt' to 'on the balance of probabilities.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: Assuming an independent judiciary, THS granting courts in liberal democracies the power to bar certain parties and candidates from running for office on the basis that them holding office would pose significant/substantial threats to democracy.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THW introduce a criminal liability for failure to render assistance.
Under German criminal law, a person can be held criminally liable for 'Unterlassene Hilfeleistung' (Failure to render assistance). This is when an individual does not render assistance in the case of an accident or an emergency although it is necessary and can reasonably be expected under the circumstances, in particular if it is possible without substantial danger to that person and without breaching other important duties.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
“PROTECTOR” – Assuming a tradeoff, TH, as the United Earth Government, W pursue the Digital Life Project over the Moving Mountain Project.
In the far future, the Sun has become an expanding red giant and is threatening to engulf the Earth in 100 years. Realizing the impending danger, the world's countries have come together to form a United Earth Government, pooling their resources to save humanity. One proposed plan, known as the Moving Mountain Project, involves constructing thousands of gigantic ion engines to propel the Earth out of the Solar System towards another habitable star system. A second proposed plan, the Digital Life Project, involves developing mind uploading technologies to achieve a future of "digital immortality". All human consciousnesses will be uploaded onto a supercomputer that will be launched into the cosmos, and all future humans will live in a virtual, simulated consciousness. Both plans have been assessed to be possible, but the Digital Life Project has been giving a decently higher chance of succeeding.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
“Me, Myself, and I” – THP the rational conceptualization of identity to the atomized conceptualization.
There are two broad ways of describing personal identity: relational and atomized. The relational self is understood as it relates to others. The nature of an individual is determined by how that individual stands in relation to others. The atomized self is one where personal identity is described as discrete and self-contained. An atomized self may interact and cooperate with others but each is a separate unit entire unto itself.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: 3. “Freedom” – THP anarchy to authoritarianism.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
You are an innkeeper. It is approximately 0BC. The King has issued an edict requiring all newborns to be handed to his soldiers. If you are found in possession of a newborn, you will be killed. You know of such a newborn; he is in your inn’s stable. You suspect the child may be sacred to your God.
THW not turn in the child.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
Assuming you’ll never be able to find the community again, TH, as Slobodan, W stay in the community with Ana instead of continuing the journey and trying to facilitate the creation of this vaccine.
You are Slobodan, a man living in a post-apocalyptic world full of zombies. You accept an offer to take an orphaned girl, Ana, who is immune to the zombie infection, to a group of revolutionaries who may be able to create a vaccine against the infection, and in return they will reward you richly. You have promised your romantic partner that you will bring the girl to her destination, but before the most dangerous part of the road you come across a hidden and successful community surrounded by walls, where a normal and safe life is currently being lived, and one of the residents is your brother.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THP a world that includes the star chamber.
The star chamber is a supernatural power who randomly but accurately identifies a small percentage of immoral action that would otherwise go unpunished, and then ensures that the perpetrators of these acts suffer to a degree that is proportionate to their offense. This punishment is administered in such a way that no one (including the perpetrator) ever connects it to the immoral actions. The chamber’s existence is public.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Shreya Ranjan, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
TH, as the Chinese Communist Party, W allow farmers in rural areas to freely sell their homes and land.
In rural areas in China, land is owned by the “collective” (the village). Rural households have a right to use a piece of land to build their homes, but may not sell their property to people elsewhere. Currently, rural China is extremely poor, and millions of rural farmers have already moved to overcrowded urban areas where they struggle to set up given a lack of capital.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THBT developing countries should adopt Germany’s "debt brake."
Germany’s “debt brake” restricts its government’s borrowing to 0.35% of GDP per year. The rule can only be suspended in exceptional emergencies (e.g. the COVID-19 pandemic, energy crisis).
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: THBT North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) should expand its collective defense obligations to include non-military provocations (e.g. airspace violations, cyberattacks, etc. )
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
TH, as the leadership of a major news organisation (e.g., The Economist, CBS, The Washington Post), would adopt moralized journalism as a stance.
Moralized journalism refers to media outlets taking an explicit moral or values-driven approach in their reporting. Such outlets would take a clear and consistent stance on the issues they cover. This may include coverage that emphasises moral outrage, the use of emotionally charged language, and links to broader social narratives.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THP a world where most film is neo-realist.
Neo-realist cinema focuses on stories of ordinary people and their everyday lives. Neo-realism also prioritizes authenticity, often with loose structure and observational storytelling, over highly stylised narratives. Neo-realist film is usually low-budget and shot on location with non-professional actors, using natural light and sound.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: THS the glorification of 'authentic' tourist experiences that aim to reflect local life and culture.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: TH, as an average Gen Z person in a developed country, W lead a life heavily prioritising flexibility and keeping options open over heavily prioritising long term commitments (people, places, vocations, etc).
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THR the social media-ification of traditional online platforms (Spotify compared to Apple Music, Goodreads compared to Kindle, etc).
For example, while Spotify and Apple Music both serve as streaming platforms, Spotify is far more profile oriented, allowing users to follow other users, post playlists, etc. Apple Music, however, is used solely as an individual platform.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: THS the significant rise in the popularity of a culture of personal exceptionalism among Gen Z (examples include a stigma around being seen as “basic” or an NPC, use of hyperspecific labels such as “cottagecore” and “e-girl,” etc.)
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: THP a world in which there are a small number of large-scale, globally-active NGOs as opposed to a large number of small-scale, locally-active NGOs.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
TH, as the disability rights movement, W prioritize a medical model of disability over a social model of disability in their advocacies.
A medical model of disability focuses on aiding or managing disability. It seeks to invest resources in healthcare to aid/manage disabilities medically to allow people with disabilities to better adjust to their circumstances. Examples include focus on prosthetics, access to therapy, and early diagnosis. A social model of disability seeks to identify and remove systemic barriers, derogatory attitudes and social exclusion of people with disabilities. Examples include focus on accessibility laws, employment protections, and anti-stigma.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: THBT courts should include the victims’ forgiveness as a mitigating factor in sentencing.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THS debt for climate swap programs.
Multilateral development banks and other multilateral organizations have been advocating for debt for climate swap programs as a debt-relief measure. Debt for Nature/Climate swaps involve reducing debt in exchange for policy commitments or spending by debtor countries.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THW replace the UN General Assembly’s one country, one vote system with a contribution-weighted voting system.
Under a “contribution-weighted voting” system, each country’s voting power would be proportional to its relative contributions to tackling global challenges. For example, if Country A contributes £5 billion, which is 5% of its GDP, and Country B contributes £10 billion, which is 1% of its GDP, then Country A would have greater voting power than Country B. Contributions can be monetary or non-monetary, for example providing soldiers for peacekeeping missions or agricultural donations.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THS private collegiate-level educational institutions adopting the Income-Share Schooling (ISS) model.
The Income-Share Schooling (ISS) model is one in which all admitted students do not pay any upfront tuition or fees. The private educational institution covers the cost of a students’ learning for all levels they offer and that the student chooses to take. After completing their education and earning above a minimum income threshold, they must pay a fixed percentage of their income for a set period of time under an income-share contract. The specific contractual terms (e.g. repayment rate, threshold, penalties) are set at the discretion of each institution.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
​​In times of crisis, THBT central banks should use their direct credit allocation mechanisms to provide sectors with high positive externalities* easier credit access (*e.g., green tech, infrastructure, healthcare)
During crises, commercial banks and firms may struggle to obtain market credit. In such situations, central banks may use direct credit allocation mechanisms, such as providing loans to banks or, more rarely, directly to firms. These tools allow central banks to channel cheaper credit to specific sectors (e.g., by offering discounted funding to banks conditional on lending to targeted sectors, by lending directly to firms in that sector, or through sector-specific bond purchases).
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: THBT states should heavily tax companies whose technologies significantly reduce human labor demand.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: THBT liberal democracies should, in strategic sectors, prefer taking equity stakes over providing subsidies to companies.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: TH, as the founder of a new religion with a good/bad afterlife (e.g. Heaven/Hell, Jannah/Jahannam, the Good Place/the Bad Place etc.), W adopt a view of the “bad” afterlife that allows souls to move to the “good” afterlife after death, as opposed to the view that a soul’s afterlife is solely determined by their life.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THP a notion of happiness guided by ataraxia rather than one guided by eudaimonia.
For Aristotle, happiness, called eudaimonia, is the ultimate goal of human life. It is achieved when the individual lives according to reason and practices virtues such as justice, courage, and temperance. To be happy, in this sense, is to fulfill one's moral and rational potential, contributing to the common good. For Pyrrho, happiness is achieved through ataraxia, a state of inner tranquility and emotional imperturbability. It arises when the individual suspends judgment about what is "good" or "bad," accepting the world as it is and not depending on acting morally or rationally, but on cultivating detachment.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THBT AGI models should follow virtue ethics over deontology.
Artificial general intelligence (AGI) is a hypothetical stage in which an AI system can match or exceed the cognitive abilities of human beings across nearly all tasks. Deontology is a moral system that states there are certain rules which are absolute and should never be breached (e.g. murder, stealing, etc.). Virtue ethics is a moral system that states that to be moral, a person should have virtuous characteristics. These characteristics are a moderation of two vices. For example, between the extremes of cowardice and hubris, individuals should find the virtue of being brave.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: THR narratives that claim that history inevitably moves towards a particular end (e.g. Fukuyama's "end of history", Marx's "global revolution").
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: THP a world where the primary narrative about love is that it is a choice.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: THO the narrative that “everyone has their own truth.”
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
TH, as the animal rights movement, regret the dominance of anthropocentrism in discussions about animal rights.
Anthropocentrism is the doctrine that humans are the center of all things and are the most important entities in the universe. This doctrine is often used to evaluate or interpret the world based on human values and experiences. In discussions about animal rights, anthropocentrism is often manifested through human evaluations of animal rights based on human-like values (e.g., intelligence, sentience, etc.) and human moral scales. In contrast to anthropocentrism, other doctrines exist such as ecocentrism, which posits that humans are merely an equal part of the web of life. According to this view, ecocentrism criticizes the prioritization of certain species because of their similarities to humans and opposes the notion that "environmental protection is for the future of our children." Instead, it argues that every component of nature has its own intrinsic value and should be respected and protected in the present, not just for the long-term benefit of humanity.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THBT strategic ambiguity in geopolitics does more harm than good.
Strategic ambiguity is a foreign policy approach where countries deliberately avoid being clear about what actions they would take in military, economic, or diplomatic situations, such as not specifying whether or how they would assist another country during a conflict or crisis — including military support, financial aid, sanctions, alliances, negotiations, or diplomatic pressure.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution: THBT environmental movements should heavily prioritize geopolitics to advocate for greener technologies and policies.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THR the popularization of RCTs in developmental economics research.
Randomized controlled trials are experiments that apply an intervention to only a randomly selected portion of the target population, so that you can compare the effects of the intervention against a group that didn’t receive it. Since the popularization of RCTs, the field of development economics has shifted from a focus largely on nation-scale problems with no clear answers (like why countries are poor), to a focus largely on smaller problems that can be definitively answered (like whether investing in digital learning solutions in schools outperforms traditional investments).
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution: THP economics to be seen as a political field rather than a scientific field.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution: THR the increasing privatisation of the space industry.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
In places with high levels of religious radicalism, THBT schools should push progressive interpretations of religious texts as opposed to secular classes on ethics and morality.
Religious radicalism involves literalist, often extreme interpretations of faith for the purpose of enforcing religious law.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
THP a world where people who care about creating change in the world primarily follow the 'earn to give model' instead of 'working to maximize social good'.
The "earn-to-give model" is a philosophy/movement that suggests the best strategy for people who want to create a change in the world is to earn a substantial income and then donate a significant portion of it to cost- effective charities. On the other hand, "work that maximizes social good" focuses on how you can create a positive impact on people through your work in various ways, such as social entrepreneurship or working in non-profit organizations. Usually, those working to maximize social good choose sectors that are less profitable and earn less money than the earn-to-give model.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
THW establish life on the paradise planet.
You and a group of scientists are sent into outer space on a sub-light speed ship for the mission to resettle Earth after the effects of climate change subsides. Some humans remained on Earth, and while human societies survived, civilization did not, returning human activity on Earth to a primitive state. While waiting for climate change to expire, you have ventured around the galaxy and found an uninhabited paradise planet that seems better able to provide for society than earth. Due to the nature of sub-light-speed travel, your crew only aged a few years even though a millennia passed on Earth (and all of your commanders have died). You now have two exclusive choices; to return to Earth and fulfill your mission or to abandon your mission and establish life on the paradise planet.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
THR the decline of the humanities in higher education.
In academic settings, “the humanities” is contrasted with the study of natural sciences, social sciences, and professional training. Core fields in the humanities include: literature, philosophy, history, religion, art.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
THW mandate all scientists to periodically conduct reproducibility research. (e.g. reproducing the result of existing research or experiments meta-analysis of reproducibility of pre-existing experiments)
Reproducibility is a major principle of the scientific method. It means when different researchers follow the same methodology of a research (i.e. experiments or observational study) to reproduce the result it should match the result of the original research. Currently many research results in some fields are said to be unreproducible especially in several branches of Biology Psychology and Behavioral Economics etc.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
THS the rise of Sci-Hub.
Sci-Hub is a website that provides free access to millions of paywalled and open-access research papers and books. Sci-Hub obtains paywalled papers by presenting compromised university usernames and passwords to the publishers' websites via the universities' proxy websites. The means by which Sci-Hub obtains usernames and passwords are legal and illegal.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution: TH, as a prominent progressive journalist in an authoritarian state, W shift their efforts on informal journalism (e.g., citizen journalism, hyper-local reporting) as opposed to working within large traditional media agencies.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution: In developed countries, THP fierce polarization over widespread political apathy.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution: TH, as an opposition party that just came to power against an incumbent party who held on to power for a long time, W reject members of the previous ruling party who seek to “party hop” (switch) to TH’s party.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
Assuming feasibility, THP a world with the TrustHuman system.
In the TrustHuman system, people can submit a numerical review of their interactions with others. Every person then has a personal, with a weighted system where long-term interactions are weighed more than short-term interactions (e.g. a review from one's long term partner would have a larger effect than a review from a waiter in a restaurant one has visited once). People are allowed to change their rating of others if circumstances change, and you can only rate people you have personally interacted with. Everyone's overall rating is publicly accessible, but one cannot access each individual rating. Each person's rating is only accessible once there is a sufficient number of reviews.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
THR the Goldwater Rule.
Since 1973, the American Psychiatric Association’s Goldwater Rule has instructed psychiatrists not to speculate on the mental health of public figures whom they have not examined. The Rule is widely-followed, and similar norms exist among other mental health professionals.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
THBT the feminist movement should actively frame consumers of the manosphere as victims rather than ideological opponents.
The manosphere is a loose network of online communities, blogs, and influencers centered on men’s issues, often promoting masculinity and traditional gender roles to an extreme degree.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution: THBT the response to global pandemics should be led by the World Health Organization rather than by individual governments.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
Assuming the US successfully invades Greenland without casualties on either side, TH, as the EU, W choose a passive response rather than an aggressive response.
An aggressive response to an invasion includes enacting sweeping sanctions, expelling diplomats, military action and abandoning bi- and multilateral collaboration. A passive response to an invasion may instead include seeking agreement through negotiations and mediation.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
THP a widespread adoption of process-based as opposed to outcomes-based environmental regulation.
Process-based environmental regulations refers to regulations that prescribe specific practices (i.e. use of renewable energy sources, specific farming techniques to reduce emission, etc.) in protecting the environment. In comparison, outcomes-based regulation (e.g. emission caps or waste reduction goals) without prescribing how they must be achieved.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution: THP art that addresses class inequality through the glorification of working-class lives (e.g. Paterson, Empire of Light, American Beauty, Take Me Home, Country Roads) over art that addresses class inequality through the demonisation of economic elites (e.g. The Wolf of Wall Street, The Great Gatsby, Snowpiercer, American Psycho).
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
THO the trend of prioritising technical elements over program components in figure skating scoring.
The ISU (Ice Skating Union) recently changed its judging system. The new system rewards skaters for performing technical elements (complicated jumps or turns, etc...) over program elements (artistic expression), The move was intended to make the sport more objective and offer more transparency in how skaters are judged.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
On Tuesday, OpenAI shut down its video creation platform Sora, claiming it was a “distraction” from focusing on corporate products such as software generation tools.
THO the increasing trend of AI companies shifting focus from consumer-facing products to corporate clients.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
THW join the protests.
You are a progressive, only child of a wealthy political dynasty, who recently graduated from university and are expected to inherit your family’s power. A large corruption scandal has emerged around infrastructure projects linked to your relatives. Despite growing public outrage and investigations, your family has denied all allegations, refused cooperation, and is likely to get away with it. Your attempts to persuade them to cooperate have failed. Inside the household, relatives insist on denying the allegations and waiting for the controversy to pass. You are expected to stay quiet and protect your eventual succession, with the understanding that you could set a different course once you are in charge. At the same time, protests continue to grow, creating the possibility of distancing yourself from your family now and building legitimacy as their eventual successor.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
TH, as the young alien superhuman, W choose option B over option A.
You are a young alien superhuman living on Earth. You were raised by a kind and grounded human family who taught you the value of humility, everyday life, and trust in human systems. You’ve grown up believing in the importance of restraint : that your powers should be used only when absolutely necessary, and always within the boundaries of law and society. Recently, you’ve discovered messages left by your biological alien father. He believes that Earth is unstable and that your superior abilities give you a duty to rise above others to guide, protect, and shape civilization. He urges you to embrace your potential by taking leadership and shaping the world around you. You now have two options: Option A, where you continue to live as a normal human who occasionally uses your powers to help others, or Option B, whereby you attempt to take a major proactive role in reshaping and altering existing power structures.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
TH, as the engineer, W choose to destroy the machine.
You are an engineer living in a liberal democracy. Your corporation has secretly developed a machine that can predict the future of all newborn children, such as whether or not the child grows up to be a serial killer or a doctor who can cure cancer. The machine’s predictions so far have never failed, despite hundreds of tests. The government gets wind of this invention and compels you to hand over this machine.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Dari Zell, Andy Montvelisky

Resolution:
THBT countries in West and Central Africa should abandon the CFA Franc.
The CFA Franc is the name for two currencies used in 14 countries across West and Central Africa. The system dates back to 1945, when France created the “Franc of the French Colonies in Africa” to manage the economies of its colonies. After gaining independence in the 1960s, most of these countries chose to keep using the CFA Franc. The Franc has since been divided into two regional groups, one for West Africa and one for Central Africa, and each group has its own central bank. The CFA Franc operates through four main features. First, its value is pegged to the Euro at a rate of 1 Euro = 656 CFA Francs. Second, France guarantees that the CFA Franc can always be exchanged for Euros, which helps keep it stable. Third, every member country must keep half of its foreign currency reserves in a special account at the French Treasury. Fourth, French government representatives sit on the boards of both African central banks and have the power to veto important monetary decisions.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Alexander Georgatos

Resolution:
THP “tech over truth” judging to “truth over tech” judging.
In competitive debate, judges often adopt different philosophies about how to evaluate arguments. Two common approaches are “tech over truth” and “truth over tech.” For a judge utilizing a “tech over truth” approach, an argument is considered "true for the round" if the argument is (1) adequately warranted/justified and (2) insufficiently rebutted. This holds irrespective of the "real-world truth" of the argument. For a judge utilizing a “truth over tech” approach, an argument is considered "true for the round" if the claim is (1) adequately warranted/justified, (2) insufficiently rebutted, and (3) plausibly true for the real world.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Alexander Georgatos

Resolution:
THP authors from the African diaspora writing fiction through a lens of Afro-futurism over Afro-pessimism.
Both Afro-futurism and Afro-pessimism refer to approaches in exploring African experiences through different cultural aesthetics. Afro-futurism reimagines the future via a black lens, voiding white supremacist thought and the structures that violently oppressed Black communities. Afro-futurism evaluates the past and future to create better conditions for the present generation of Black people through the use of advanced technology, often presented through art, music, and literature. An example of Afro-futurist literature is Wakanda (Marvel) and Beyonce's Lemonade (2016). Alternatively, Afro-pessimism is a lens of interpretation that accounts for, and focuses on the ongoing effects of racism, colonialism, and historical processes of enslavement including the trans-Atlantic slave trade and their impact on structural conditions as well as the personal, subjective, and lived experience and embodied reality of African Americans. An example of Afro-pessimism literature is Twelve Years A Slave.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Alexander Georgatos

Resolution:

For the purposes of the debate, the label is giving you whatever resources you need (equipment, advisors, etc.) and general creative freedom over the kind of album you make. A concept album is an album with a strong and clear cohesive theme, which can also be a consistent recurring motif, where later songs on the album frequently call back to and reference lyrics and moments on earlier songs. Lorde’s Melodrama is an example of this where every song is a song describing what it’s like to be at a specific party. GNX by Kendrick Lamar is an example of a non-concept album.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Alexander Georgatos

Resolution:
THS the repatriation of the Parthenon Marbles to Greece.
The Parthenon Marbles, also known as the Elgin Marbles, are a collection of classical Greek sculptures and architectural pieces originally part of the Parthenon and other buildings on the Acropolis of Athens. Between 1801 and 1812, Lord Elgin, the British ambassador to the Ottoman Empire (which then ruled Greece), removed roughly half of the surviving sculptures and transported them to Britain. In 1816, the British Museum acquired these marbles, where they remain on public display today. The British Museum argues that the collection was legally obtained with permission from the Ottoman authorities, that it has been preserved and made accessible to a global audience, and that it forms part of a universal museum context representing world heritage.The Greek government, however, maintains that the marbles were taken under foreign occupation and therefore without legitimate consent. Greece has built the Acropolis Museum in Athens to house the sculptures in a controlled environment near their original site and has repeatedly requested their return.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Alexander Georgatos

Resolution:
THR the U.S. government’s 2008 financial bailout of major banks.
In 2008, the United States entered a massive financial crisis—widely understood as primarily caused by major banks. Throughout the early 2000s, banks engaged in increasingly risky behavior: they issued subprime mortgages to borrowers who were unlikely to repay; bundled these risky loans into complex financial products like mortgage-backed securities (investments made up of many home loans packaged together and sold to investors, who in turn receive the homeowners’ monthly mortgage payments) and sold them across the global financial system while misrepresenting their safety. Banks also relied on extremely high levels of leverage, meaning they borrowed heavily to increase profits, which made them more vulnerable when asset prices fell. When homeowners began defaulting in large numbers, these mortgage-linked assets collapsed in value. Because banks were deeply invested in and interconnected through these products, the losses spread rapidly across the financial sector. This led to the failure of major institutions like Lehman Brothers, emergency rescues of Bear Stearns and AIG, and a global freezing of credit markets. At its peak, the U.S. was losing hundreds of thousands of jobs per month, unemployment climbed to 10%., millions of homeowners faced foreclosure, and stock markets plummeted, shrinking retirement accounts, pensions, and savings. To prevent the collapse of the financial system, the U.S. government enacted the Troubled Asset Relief Program (TARP) in 2008, authorizing $700 billion to stabilize major banks. The bailout injected capital into banks, purchased distressed assets, and provided emergency loans.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Alexander Georgatos

One of the most significant measures taken was the complete halt of Russian oil imports. Due to this, the price of Russian Oil significantly decreased. In August 2025, the US increased its sanctions on India by 25% as a result of their continued buying of Russian oil.

Resolution:

Typically included in bilateral or multilateral investment treaties, investor-state dispute settlement (ISDS) provisions enable foreign investors to bypass local courts and resolve disputes with states through a binding decision from an independent tribunal. Through ISDS provisions, investors may seek arbitration against a host state for actions that breach fair treatment, violate contractual obligations, or otherwise harm their investments. ‘Right to regulate’ clauses in ISDS provisions recognise a state’s right to make laws in the public interest—such as those for health, environmental protection, land and indigenous rights, or labour standards—without risking investor claims  for compensation.

Resolution:
For famous historical artworks where traditional restorations would be unremovable (permanently alter the painting), THP Kachkine’s method of restoration to traditional methods.
Traditional restoration involves experts using chemical, X-ray, and UV analysis, along with a detailed knowledge of art history and chemistry, to determine what in a painting needs restoration. An expert then manually adds adhesive to chipping paint and paints sections where the original paint has been chipped away. MIT researcher Alex Kachkine has developed a new method to restore paintings. He scans paintings and applies AI algorithms to create digitally restored versions, mapping thousands of regions needing repair and identifying precise colors. This data is printed onto masks that are varnished onto the painting to reproduce the painting’s original appearance. These masks are fully removable, and a digital file of the exact changes are stored.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Informal evaluation takes a holistic approach, looking at whether an employee’s general work ethic and performance meet expectations, but without relying on specific metrics. By contrast, Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) use structured, results-driven measures, such as meeting sales targets or completing a set number of projects.

Resolution:
THBT medical institutions should adopt the progressive qualification criteria as opposed to the classical qualification criteria when awarding treatment.
Classical qualification criteria refer to the pre-established medical metrics used to determine which patients should receive treatments that are resource- or cost-intensive. These criteria generally aim to prioritise patients with the highest likelihood of a good prognosis and meaningful extension of life. For example, in organ transplantation, candidates with active infections, cancer, advanced age, or severe comorbidities are typically excluded. Progressive qualification criteria refer to medical decision-making frameworks that focus on broader social and ethical considerations when allocating resource-intensive treatments. Under this approach, priority might be given to individuals who play a significant role in supporting or uplifting their communities, or to those with multiple dependents who would be severely affected by their loss.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

As a result, many schools face difficulties maintaining class sizes, offering specialist subjects, and ensuring continuity of teaching staff. Funding for teacher recruitment goes toward scholarships, hiring incentives, recruitment campaigns, certification programs, mentorship, and university partnerships to attract, train, and retain qualified teachers.

Resolution:
THP a world where a countercyclical global insurance fund is the main source of credit of nations as opposed to the Bretton-Woods Institutions.
The Bretton Woods Institutions, mainly the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank, were established after World War II to promote global financial stability and development. Both institutions are funded by member countries: wealthier nations contribute more and hold greater voting power. The IMF provides short-term financial assistance and policy advice to countries facing debt crises, often with strict conditions requiring austerity or structural reforms. The World Bank focuses on long-term development loans and infrastructure projects, funded through member contributions and global capital markets. A countercyclical global insurance fund, by contrast, is a system where countries contribute to a shared pool during economic growth periods, and the fund provides financial support to nations in recession, stabilizing economies without the same political or policy conditions attached.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:
THR the Norm of Bilingualism in Canadian Politics.
Canada was colonized first by the French in the 17th Century, and later more expansively by the British. The Canadian Province of Quebec, the territory most heavily colonized by the French, has long held a unique identity centered around the primacy of the French language and has historically suffered from both a relative lack of economic development and from discrimination against Francophones in the labor market. Approximately a quarter of all Canadians are Francophones and over 80% of these Francophones reside in the Province of Quebec. Since 1969, Canada has officially been bilingual at the federal level, and all federal laws and services are available in both French and English. Lingering tensions led Quebec to hold referendums on secession in 1980 and 1995, both of which had a small majority voting against secession. Today, Quebec and Francophones hold an outsize role in Canadian politics, with half of Prime Ministers hailing from Quebec since 1968 and all being bilingual. There is a strong norm - enforced by some parties as rule - that candidates for party leadership and by extension federal premiership be bilingual.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Dari Zell, Annabel Gottlieb (equity)

Resolution:
THBT governments should prioritize supply-side solutions over demand-side and redistributive solutions to address housing crises in major cities.
In many major cities, housing costs have risen rapidly due to population growth, urbanization, and limited new development. Economists broadly agree that two leading strategies can reduce housing costs: Supply-side solutions aim to increase the total number of housing units, primarily through zoning reform, easing building restrictions, and incentivising private development. Demand-side and redistributive solutions aim to help individuals afford housing through government interventions such as rent control, public/social housing, rental subsidies, or taxes on vacant homes. While both approaches can coexist, governments often have limited resources and must prioritize one approach in both funding and political capital.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
In a liberal democracy, THP Norway’s trial system for major crimes to the US’.
In Norway, serious criminal cases often use “lay judges,” who sit with professional judges. Higher courts typically have three professionals and four lay judges. Lay judges are ordinary citizens appointed for four-year terms by (elected) municipal councils, and are supposed to reflect the country’s demographics. They deliberate and vote with professional judges on both guilt and sentencing. Decisions are made by majority vote. Most lay judges can expect to hear only a few cases during their four-year term. In the US, juries of 12 randomly selected citizens decide only on guilt or liability, deliberating separately from the judge until they reach a unanimous decision. Judges oversee procedure and determine sentencing (except in limited death penalty cases). In both countries, except for special circumstances, you cannot opt out of serving as a lay judge/juror, but those determined to have special interests in a trial that prevent their decisions from being unbiased cannot serve as a lay judge/juror for that trial.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Zoe Manges, Alex Doft, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
TH, as a popular and expanding new fast food chain, W adopt the franchise system over the corporate system.
A franchise is a type of license that grants a franchisee access to a franchisor's proprietary business knowledge, processes, reputation and trademarks, thus allowing the franchisee to sell a product or service under the franchisor's business name (e.g. most McDonalds’ and Taco bells are franchises). In exchange for acquiring a franchise, the franchisee usually pays the franchisor an initial start-up fee and annual licensing fees. On the other hand, a corporation is a distinct legal entity that is owned by its shareholders and managed by a board of directors. The board makes major business decisions, sets company policy, and oversees the corporation’s officers. Unlike a franchise, a corporation runs all of its business outlets (e.g. this is how Shake Shack and Chipotle operate).

TH, as the founder of a new religion, W choose to use the Revelation Model over the Closed-Canon Model.
The Closed-Canon Model sees religious doctrine as fixed, based on a closed canon of scripture, and any developments are through interpretation rather than new revelations. For example, the Roman Catholic Church considers the Bible and teachings of the early Church Fathers as complete, and that public revelation ended with the death of the last apostle. The Revelation Model allows new beliefs to be introduced through revelations received by living prophets and apostles. Doctrine can evolve over time, and new scriptures can be added to the canon. For instance, the process by which the Mormon Church accepts new beliefs involves revelation to the modern day prophets and apostles, council decision as to whether to present the revelation, communication of the revelation to the Church, member acceptance through sustaining votes, and subsequent implementation.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Andy Montvelisky, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
Assuming feasibility, THS the transfer of control of the US federal funds rate from the FOMC to a specially built AI model whose goal is to maintain a 2% inflation rate.
The Federal Reserve is the central banking system of the United States. The Fed considers itself “independent” because its decisions do not have to be approved by anyone and the terms of the members of the board of governors are 14 years long. It is governed by the presidentially appointed board of governors. Twelve regional banks regulate private banks. The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) sets the US federal funds rate, which influences market interest rates, in an attempt to stabilize inflation at its target of 2%. The FOMC consists of the board of governors and the regional bank presidents. The FOMC has been criticized for its inflation management, perceived lack of transparency, and role in economic downturns. Trump has recently pressured Fed Chair Jerome Powell to cut interest rates, criticized his leadership, and nominated allies to succeed him when he is “out of there.” Fed board members can only be removed “for cause,” traditionally interpreted as misconduct or incapacity, and Powell has maintained a commitment to independence.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Andy Montvelisky, Maliha Thomas (equity)

Resolution:
THBT the EU should stop the implementation of CBAM.
The EU currently has an EU-wide carbon cap with an emissions trading market. A Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) is a policy designed to encourage global adoption of carbon reduction practices and protect domestic industries from unfair competition due to differing environmental standards. It does so by adjusting the carbon cost of imported goods produced with high emissions, like steel or cement. Importers calculate the product’s embedded carbon and pay a fee (to the EU) equivalent to the EU carbon price. For example, if producing imported steel emitted 2 tons of CO₂ per ton of steel, and the EU carbon price is €50/ton, the importing country must pay the EU €100 per ton of steel as a CBAM fee. If the exporting country already taxed the good’s emissions, the CBAM fee is reduced to avoid double taxation. The EU is implementing CBAM, applying in its definitive regime from 2026, while the current transitional phase lasts between 2023 and 2025. Yet, BRICs countries and other developing countries have shown resistance to CBAM.
Topic Committee: Ethan Bordoff, Jane Kelly, Andy Montvelisky, Maliha Thomas (equity)

PAFA chose to keep their Close exhibition displayed, adding placards explaining and condemning his behaviour, and creating a space where visitors could write their thoughts about issues of gender and accountability in the arts. They also organised an exhibition by female artists to sit alongside Close’s work. PAFA continues to teach Close’s work in its curriculum. Conversely, Seattle responded by removing Close’s exhibition and no longer teaching or displaying his work.

THO academic activism.
Academic activism refers to a phenomenon where academics take an explicitly political standpoint in their work and outside it with the aim of bringing about social change. Activist academics openly associate with different social and political groups or movements. Examples include Judith Butler (American philosopher and gender theorist, and active in the Occupy movement), Taimur Rahman (Professor at the Lahore University of Management Sciences, and Secretary-General of the Mazdoor Kisan Party [communist political party]).

THS intellectual banality as a key principle in the academic approach.
Intellectual banality, in this debate, is defined as a principle that states the practices and habits of academia should make intellectual discourse (e.g., debates, lectures, academic forums) and academic products (e.g., journals, theses, dissertations, papers, etc.) more banal, meaning simple and easily understood by the general public, both in terms of substance and communication.

THBT Hogwarts should allow students to opt out of House affiliation.
Throughout the academic year, students earn or lose House Points based on their behaviour, academic performance, and conduct. Professors and staff award or deduct points at their discretion. Points can be given for acts such as answering questions correctly, showing bravery, or exemplary performance. Points can be deducted for rule-breaking, poor behaviour, or failure to follow instructions. At the end of the year, the House with the highest number of points is awarded the House Cup, a prestigious honour that fosters competition between Houses.

THBT the person to be sacrificed should be chosen by lottery instead of by majority vote.
You are part of the first manned mission to settle Mars. After an accident, all contact with Earth is lost and supplies have become so depleted that the only way for anyone to survive is to sacrifice one member of the 10 person group.

THP developing the technology to terraform other planets in our solar system over searching for habitable exoplanets.
Terraforming is an as yet unproven scientific concept that tries to change the environment of other planets to make them more like Earth. This involves changing the planet's atmosphere, temperature, and surface to make it habitable. For example, on Mars, terraforming would involve warming the planet up and adding oxygen to its atmosphere, so humans could breathe without space suits. An exoplanet is a planet outside our solar system.
Assuming Mars has been terraformed and is ready to house human civilization, THW require that all the settlers on Mars be atheists.

THW introduce a 'youthocratic' voting system in functional democracies.
A "youthocratic" voting system grants more weight to votes cast by the youth (e.g. the weightage assigned to a vote is inversely proportional to age of the individuals). For the purposes of the debate, the minimum legal age to vote is 21.

Assuming feasibility, THW elect the Pope through direct voting by all catholics instead of the papal conclave.
The papal conclave is the process by which the college of cardinals of the Catholic church elects a new pope.
Assuming empathy could be accurately measured, THW restrict voting to individuals with a high level of empathy for those outside of their immediate circle.

THBT climate activists should prioritize funding the development of clean technology rather than increasing regulation of the fossil fuel industry.
For this debate, efforts to address climate change can take two broad forms. One approach emphasizes advancing low-emission technologies such as renewable energy, storage, carbon capture, and electrification, with the aim of making clean options more cost-effective and scalable. Another approach emphasizes limiting the use of high-emission energy sources through tools like carbon pricing, emissions caps, subsidy reform, or restrictions on extraction and consumption.

THBT individuals should have the right to sell their organs.
Currently, in most countries, the sale of human organs is prohibited, though donation is permitted under regulated systems. Allowing sale would mean individuals could receive financial compensation for providing organs such as kidneys, rather than relying solely on voluntary donation.

As a democracy, THW make voting compulsory.
In many democracies, voting is voluntary, though citizens are encouraged to participate. Under compulsory voting, eligible citizens are legally required to vote, with penalties such as fines or civic sanctions for non-participation. Some countries, like Australia and Belgium, already use this system.

THS rewilding land.
“Rewilding” refers to the process of restoring land to its natural, unmanaged state, often by reducing human use, reintroducing native species, and allowing ecosystems to regenerate with minimal intervention. Examples include returning farmland to forests or grasslands, or reintroducing predators like wolves to restore ecological balance.
In countries with compulsory military service for men, THW advocate for compulsory conscription for women too.
THW allow terminally ill patients to access experimental drugs without regulatory approval.

THBT climate refugees should be granted the same legal protections as political refugees.
International law, such as the 1951 Refugee Convention, grants protections to people fleeing persecution based on factors like race, religion, nationality, or political opinion. People displaced by climate-related events, such as rising sea levels, desertification, or extreme weather, are not currently covered under these protections, and are often classified as migrants rather than refugees.
THBT space exploration should prioritize finding new, habitable planets over finding pools of resources for extraction.

THR the normalization of “hustle culture” in youth-oriented media.
“Hustle culture” refers to the idea that constant productivity, long working hours, and turning every activity into a form of self-improvement or income are necessary for success. In youth-oriented media, it is often portrayed through influencers, TV shows, or online content that glorify side hustles, nonstop work, and measuring personal value through achievement.

Resolution:
US States should enact policies to promote elementary school choice.
INFO SLIDE: "School choice" programs are those which give parents more options regarding where their children go to school. The most prominent school choice program is school vouchers. In this system, each family can still enroll their child in public school as normal, but if they want to send their child to private school, the government will help them pay tuition by taking whatever money they would have spent educating the child at public school, removing that money from the public school's budget, and instead applying it to the cost of tuition at the private school of the parents' choosing. This can be summarized as "the tax dollars travel with the child."

INFO SLIDE: A secondary boycott happens when a group of employees go on strike because of a dispute with their employer, and employees of other employers refuse to work with the company whose employees are striking. For example, when employees of McDonald’s in Denmark went on strike to force McDonald’s to recognize their union, other Danish unions such as dock workers and truck drivers refused to unload or deliver McDonald’s cargo, even though the dock workers and truck drivers were not McDonald’s employees.

Resolution:
The "pink tide" in Latin America has done more harm than good.
INFO SLIDE: The "pink tide" is a turn towards leftist political movements in Latin American nations in the late 20th and 21st centuries. The dissolution of the Soviet Union allowed leftist parties to operate independent of fears of Soviet control, and reduced the intensity of anti-communist intervention from the United States. Many leftist parties took this opportunity to consolidate power. Economic difficulties across Latin America intensified this process in the 21st century. Many argue that the death of Hugo Chavez marked the end to this trend, and by the mid-2010s, Latin American governments began to move rightward.

Resolution:
Capitalism is failing into techno-feudalism.
INFOSLIDE: Techno-feudalism is a concept that describes the shift from a capitalist economy to a system where a few dominant technology corporations and digital platforms exert feudal-like control over markets, labor, and data. In this model, wealth and power are concentrated in the hands of tech giants that act as digital landlords, extracting rents from users, workers, and smaller businesses through platform fees, data monetization, and algorithmic control. Unlike traditional capitalism, where competition and production drive profits, techno-feudalism relies on monopolistic dominance, network effects, and surveillance capitalism to maintain control.

INFO SLIDE: Hedonism prioritizes pleasure as the highest good and primary motivation for human actions. Hedonists believe that a fulfilling life is one where personal enjoyment is paramount. Asceticism emphasises self-discipline and the renunciation of physical pleasures. Ascetics believe that restraint from indulgence leads to a more meaningful and fulfilling life.

Resolution:
THP rule-based utilitarianism to act-based utilitarianism.
INFO SLIDE: Act utilitarianism says that when faced with a choice, you should ask what choice in that specific situation looks like  it will maximize the greater good, then do that. Rule utilitarianism says you should ask what rules, if followed consistently, would maximize the greater good, then follow those rules consistently even in cases where the outcome is sub-optimal. John Stuart Mill was the most famous proponent of rule utilitarianism. He cited censorship as one example where we should commit to a rule of not censoring people, arguing that following the rule consistently will get better results than allowing ourselves to censor people on a case-by-case basis.
Topic Committee: Evan Streams (chair), Rosie Bultman, Rodda John, Meghana Kandarpa, Tim Lewis, Artem Raskin

INFO SLIDE: In Mexico, anti-monuments are installed during protests to recall a tragic event or unfulfilled demand for justice. They usually critique national symbols and monuments with socio-political messaging. Examples include La Glorieta de las Mujeres que Luchan (Roundabout of the Women Who Fight), which was installed as a reminder of Mexico's gendered violence as well as the ongoing fight for equal rights, and 49 ABC, which commemorates the 49 children who died in the 2009 Hermosillo daycare center fire. The center was funded by the Mexican federal government. There are also movements to install 'positive monuments' to celebrate the legacy of minorities as a part of Mexico's national identity. Examples include a proposal to replace the Christopher Columbus statue with a replica of La joven de Amajac (The Young Women of Amajac), a pre-Hispanic sculpture depicting an indigenous woman and El Yanga, an enslaved African man who led a revolution and established a community of freed people.

On the other hand, the rank-and-file strategy involves activists getting regular, non-management jobs in key industries that could benefit from unionizing. The goal is to directly organize workers to join unions. For example, Jaz Brisack, a Rhodes Scholar, worked as a barista at Starbucks to help unionize workers there.

Although a plaintiff usually sues and wins against a company before requesting veil-piercing, plaintiffs may also request veil-piercing without suing a whole company.

Lawmakers argue that ByteDance’s ties to the Chinese government make TikTok vulnerable to foreign influence, posing a national security risk. If upheld, the ban would remove TikTok from app stores, prevent updates, and eventually degrade app functionality. Existing users may retain access temporarily, but workarounds like VPNs could bypass restrictions. ByteDance can avoid the ban by selling TikTok to a non-Chinese owner.

THBT modern day piracy, when it does not result in the physical injury or death of people, is morally justifiable.

Resolution:
THP trade conflicts between member states be resolved through regional organizations as opposed to internal arbitration within the WTO.
Article 25 of the WTO's (World Trade Organization) Dispute Settlement Understanding allows for arbitration as an alternative dispute resolution mechanism, provided both parties agree to it. This process is flexible, enabling the parties to define the scope, select arbitrators, and establish procedures. The arbitration award is binding and must be consistent with WTO agreements, with outcomes notified to the Dispute Settlement Body.
Topic Committee: Adrian Turkedjiev, Rodda John, Amber Safir, Eloise Biddle-Gottesman

Resolution:
THBT the US and Canada should ban Mandatory Binding Arbitration.
Mandatory Binding Arbitration is a contractual provision in which both parties to a contract waive their right to sue one another in court for damages related to a perceived breach of contract. Instead, in the instance of a perceived contractual violation, members must undergo arbitration, an alternative form of dispute resolution in which a mutually agreed upon private arbiter determines both the legitimacy of the alleged violation as well as the potential punishment. MBA clauses can also often include other stipulations such as waiving one's right to appeal the decision or to join class action lawsuits.
Topic Committee: Adrian Turkedjiev, Rodda John, Amber Safir, Eloise Biddle-Gottesman

Topic Committee: Adrian Turkedjiev, Rodda John, Amber Safir, Eloise Biddle-Gottesman

Resolution:
THBT religions should take a progressive revelation approach, rather than a traditional revelation approach.
Progressive revelation refers to the belief that divine truths gradually change with humanity over time, through prophets or other chosen individuals. This understanding implies that God's communication with humanity is ongoing and adaptable to the changing needs of society. For example, the prohibition on pork consumption was due to this animal being a transmitter of diseases in the past, but in the present-day, it would be morally permissible following the discovery of antibiotics. On the other hand, traditional revelation believes that God's message is eternal and does not change over time. According to this perspective, divine truths are fixed and do not change with human circumstances or social advancements. It is believed that God has transmitted complete and definitive truths through sacred scriptures or other specific revelations, and that these messages should be interpreted and followed without significant changes throughout history.
Topic Committee: Adrian Turkedjiev, Rodda John, Amber Safir, Eloise Biddle-Gottesman

TH, as Sir Gawain, W kill the dragon.
It is the age after King Arthur’s reign. There is a magical dragon up in the mountains whose breath has created a magical mist over Arthur’s kingdom. As a result of the mist, people have an extremely weak grip on their long-term memories: when separated for long periods of time, parents forget their children; lovers forgets lovers; village councils adjudicate on a problem and then have no recollection of it ever happening. A generation ago, Arthur and his knights waged a successful war to protect Britons against the Saxons and carved out a great kingdom. In order to stop future generations of Saxons from continuing the cycle of violence, the Knights slaughtered entire villages. Arthur, recognizing that this would not end the cycle of violence but rather worsen it, asked Merlin to enchant the dragon such that the memories of this genocide were lost. Today, the Britons are safe in Arthur’s kingdom and live in harmony alongside a minority of Saxons. Neighboring Saxon kingdoms look at the Saxon minorities as the best way to wage war against Arthur’s kingdom and expand. Sir Gawain, knight and nephew of Arthur, the last surviving member of the old King's faithful, walks the crypts over the skulls of Saxon babies and questions if they were in the right. Gawain is now an elderly yet formidable as ever warrior who could dispose of the dragon swiftly. Yet, so far, he has chosen not to, fearing the violence that would descend upon the land if the Saxons regained their memories of the violence.
Topic Committee: Adrian Turkedjiev, Rodda John, Amber Safir, Eloise Biddle-Gottesman

Science has now advanced to the point where a material has been developed that can pierce the dragon's scales, making it possible to launch an attack on the dragon. Resolution:
THW disallow patent rights for any technology that was developed using HeLa cells.
Henrietta Lacks died in 1951, aged 31, of an aggressive cervical cancer. Months earlier, doctors at the Johns Hopkins Hospital in Baltimore, Maryland, had taken samples of her cancerous cells while diagnosing and treating the disease. They gave some of that tissue to a researcher without Lacks’s knowledge or consent. In the laboratory, her cells turned out to have an extraordinary capacity to survive and reproduce; they were, in essence, immortal. The researcher shared them widely with other scientists, and they became a workhorse of biological research. Today, work done with HeLa cells underpins much of modern medicine; they have been involved in key discoveries in many fields, including cancer, immunology and infectious disease. One of their most recent applications has been in research for vaccines against COVID-19. Lacks was a Black woman. The hospital where her cells were collected was one of only a few that provided medical care to Black people. None of the biotechnology or other companies that profited from her cells passed any money back to her family. For decades after her death, doctors and scientists repeatedly failed to ask her family for consent as they revealed Lacks’s name publicly, gave her medical records to the media, and even published her cells’ genome online.
Topic Committee: Adrian Turkedjiev, Jiayi Liu, Ariel Segura, Zoe Manges

Former Racing 92 Neurologist Dr Chermann has come forward without consulting Sexton to say “I think Johnny Sexton has had around 30 concussions across his entire career. It’s true that it’s enormous.” “If Sexton plays on Sunday, it’s sad for him. This is what really inspires me: sadness.” Dr Barry O’Driscoll is the former medical adviser to World Rugby who stepped down in 2012 to protest against the governing body’s concussion protocols - namely the head injury assessment (HIA) and current six-day return to play protocols. There is a city called Omelas where all the citizens live perfectly fulfilled and happy lives. Buildings are beautiful, crops are plentiful, there is no disease, no crime, and no sorrow of any kind. Occasionally, a citizen, man or woman, young or old, will walk through the gate on the edge of the city. Resolution:
THP industrial unions to workplace unions.
For the purposes of this debate, a workplace union is a union that only involves workers of one specific company. These unions tend to focus on directly negotiating with the management of that individual company. By contrast, an industrial union is a union with workers from an entire industry (e.g. service workers, educators, etc.). Industrial unions, more often, focus on trying to implement industry-wide standards for worker rights rather than negotiate with individual companies.

Resolution:
THBT Brazil should declare military support for the territorial integrity of the Guyana.
In 2015, Guyana discovered large quantities of oil in the oceanic perimeter of the Essequibo region. This region occupies around 70% of Guyana's territory and is home to around 20% of the country's population, but is historically the subject of dispute with Venezuela, which has claimed the region since the end of the 18th century, claiming that the natural border between the countries is the east bank of the Essequibo River. In 1899, the US mediated an arbitration court that ceded the Essequibo region to the United Kingdom to the detriment of Venezuela, but this judgment was annulled due to procedural problems. In 1966, when Guyana became independent, the countries tried the Geneva Agreement to end the conflict, but were unable to reach a consensus. In 2018, Guyana filed a request for the International Court of Justice (ICJ) to ratify the current borders, but there has been no judgment yet. In October 2023, the Venezuelan government publicly declared its intention to annex the Essequibo region and scheduled a popular referendum to consult the Venezuelan population and decide on the annexation of the territory. The referendum is likely to have a result in favor of annexation and Venezuelan troops have already been deployed to the border.
Topic Committee: Adrian Turkedjiev, Jiayi Liu, Campbell Langdon, Maliha Thomas

European nations such as France, Ireland, and Austria have created a trade deal with Brazil and their allies known as the EU-Mercosur trade agreement, which entails major export deals and can bring 40 billion Euros of capital benefits. However, these EU nations said they will block the agreement with Brazil and other countries unless Bolsonaro agrees to do more to tackle Amazonian destruction.

Resolution:
THP atomic communitarianism to contemporary liberal democracy.
“Atomic communitarianism” refers to a system of government in which the state is divided into semi autonomous zones, organized on the basis of political philosophy. For example, an atomic communitarian civilization might be composed of a libertarian community with a fully deregulated economy and low taxes, a community that is functionally a Scandinavian-style social democracy, an anarcho-primitivist community where people live in a state of unchained nature with no laws, and more. Each community contributes financially to a central government and military force, whose only duties are to prevent communities from causing harm to each other, ensuring freedom to move from one community to another, and preventing unwanted ideological contamination (e.g., keeping televisions and cell phones out of the anarcho-primitivist community, or keeping explicit media out of the fundamentalist Christian community). The central government would allow each community to submit an information package on its values, which would be distributed to every 18-year old.

THR the notion of “YOLO.”
Info Slide: "You Only Live Once" (YOLO) is a life philosophy that emphasizes living in the present moment and taking risks, often associated with seizing opportunities and pursuing one's desires without worrying excessively about the future consequences. It suggests making the most of life's experiences, embracing spontaneity, and prioritizing personal fulfillment over caution or long-term planning.
Topic Committee: Mariel Cruz (chair), Maddie Nagle, Timothy Zhu

Resolution:
THW embrace mono no aware.
Info Slide: Mono no aware is a Japanese aesthetic that refers to the moving feeling that accompanies sensory experiences, and the knowledge that they are fundamentally transient. Specifically, mono no aware has been described as a sensitivity to beauty in bittersweet acknowledgement of its impermanence in a constantly changing world.
Topic Committee: Mariel Cruz (chair), Maddie Nagle, Timothy Zhu

Resolution:
THP a society be organized around commons, rather than private property.
Info Slide: The commons refers to a structure of holding resources/land under a communal jurisdiction, with individual usage being governed by collective norms and values. The enclosure of the commons refers to a historical process by which land held in common was claimed as private property for the exclusive use of an owner.
Topic Committee: Mariel Cruz (chair), Maddie Nagle, Timothy Zhu

Resolution:
This House believes that the US should become a unitary state.
Info Slide: In unitary states, such as France, the national government has final say on all matters of government. By contrast, the US is currently a federation, as outlined in its Constitution (see Amendment 10 and Article I Section 8). This means the federal government has final say over some matters (e.g. foreign policy), while the 50 state governments have final say over other matters (e.g. most of education policy). This balance of power between the federal and state governments has shifted throughout US history.
Topic Committee: Artem Raskin (chair), Rosie Bultman, Evan Streams, JJ Telebrico, Shujun Xiong

Resolution:

Article 21 of the German constitution states that “parties that, by reason of their aims or the behaviour of their adherents, seek to undermine or abolish the free democratic basic order or to endanger the existence of the Federal Republic of Germany shall be unconstitutional.”

But once you are inside, you may freely move between countries. If you have a visa to live and work in any Schengen country, you may freely live and work in all of them.

Resolution:
If the technology existed, this House, as a parent, would use the Child Chip.
Info slide: The Child Chip is painlessly and safely implanted into babies' brains after birth. The technology allows a parent to see the child’s location at all times and use their smartphone to see what the child is currently seeing and implement content restrictions on what the child can see (e.g., the audio would be dampened and the child would see disturbing content in real life as merely a blur). When the child’s stress levels go up, the parent will receive a notification and can alter any settings remotely. The chip will be permanently and irreversibly disabled once the child reaches 16.

Resolution:

Info Slide: For decades, El Salvador had one of the highest murder rates in the world; about 1 in 1000 El Salvadorans were murdered in 2015. This was largely due to gang violence. Nayib Bukele was elected president of El Salvador in 2019. In 2022, he declared a “state of exception,” suspending many civil liberties. Among other things, people were arrested and imprisoned en masse without trial. The murder rate has fallen by about half.

AFCTA (or the African Continental Free Trade Area) is a free trade area encompassing most of Africa. Under the agreement, AfCFTA members are committed to eliminating tariffs on most goods and services over a period of 5, 10, or 13 years, depending on the country's level of development or the nature of the products. General long-term objectives include creating a single, liberalized market; reducing barriers to capital and labor to facilitate investment; developing regional infrastructure; and establishing a continental customs union.
